title: Working with the Part That Hates Your Body
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: Learn to observe the part of you that hates your body without judgment. Understand its protective role and begin to heal the internal rift from complex trauma.
imageDescription: A pair of gentle hands hold a ceramic bowl, mended with shimmering gold, in the style of kintsugi. Warm, soft light illuminates the delicate cracks, transforming what was broken into something beautiful and strong. The scene evokes a sense of tender repair and the quiet beauty of healing.
htmlContent:
Jiddu Krishnamurti suggested that the highest form of human intelligence is to observe yourself without judgment, a practice that becomes deeply challenging when the observation is directed toward a part of us that despises the very body it inhabits. This internal friction, this wariness of our own physical form, is not a superficial dislike but a deeply rooted consequence of a nervous system that has learned to equate the body with danger, with betrayal, with a territory of memories it would rather forget. We are not talking about simple body dissatisfaction; we are talking about a fundamental rupture in the sense of self, a schism where a part of the psyche turns against its own biological foundation as a survival strategy born from complex trauma.

The Cartography of Internal Exile

When we explore the territory of complex trauma, we often find that the body became the unwilling container for experiences that were too overwhelming for the mind to process, a silent witness to events that shattered our sense of safety. The part that hates the body is not an enemy to be vanquished but a protector, a fiercely loyal guardian that concluded, at some point, that distancing from the physical self was the only way to survive the unsurvivable. This internal exile is a map of our own history, with every ache, every numb spot, and every jolt of revulsion a landmark pointing back to an original wound. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen how this part often holds the very essence of the trauma... the terror, the helplessness, the rage - encapsulated and walled off from conscious awareness as a significant act of self-preservation. We might find ourselves navigating a world where we feel disconnected from our limbs, where we look in the mirror and see a stranger, or where we experience our own physical needs as a source of irritation and threat. This is the logic of a nervous system shaped by trauma, a logic that prioritizes safety above all else, even if that safety comes at the cost of connection to our own form. The work, then, is not to force a reunion but to understand the architecture of the exile itself, to approach this alienated part with a curiosity that doesn't demand immediate change. It is about learning to read the language of our own internal terrain, a language spoken not in words but in the felt sense of the body itself.

The Observer and the Observed

The Body as a Communicator

The Practice of Embodiment

Here is the thing, though.

The Illusion of the Separate Self

At the heart of the conflict between the part that hates the body and the body itself is a fundamental misunderstanding about the nature of the self. We have been conditioned to believe that we are a separate, isolated entity, a "me" that is distinct from our thoughts, our feelings, and our physical form. This belief in a separate self is the root of so much of our suffering, for it creates a sense of alienation and fragmentation, a feeling of being at war with ourselves. The contemplative traditions, however, offer a different view. They suggest that the self is not a fixed and solid entity but a fluid and dynamic process, a constantly changing flow of experience. From this perspective, the part that hates the body is not a separate "thing" but a pattern of thought, a constellation of sensations, a wave in the vast ocean of consciousness. To work with this part is not to try to get rid of it but to see it for what it is: an impersonal process, a conditioned response, a sign of past pain. What we find here is not to say that we are not responsible for our actions; rather, it is to recognize that the sense of a separate self who is "in charge" is an illusion. The struggle ceases. The internal war comes to an end. We realize that we are not the part that hates the body, nor are we the body that is hated. We are the awareness in which both the hater and the hated appear. This is a significant and liberating insight, one that has the power to transform our entire experience of life. It is the realization that our true nature is not limited to the small, constricted sense of self that we have taken ourselves to be but is as vast and open as the sky itself. It is from this place of spacious awareness that we can begin to hold all the parts of ourselves with an unconditional love, a love that does not depend on whether they are "good" or "bad," "healed" or "unhealed." It is a love that is our very essence, the ground of our being, the source of all true healing. For a deeper dive into this topic, consider reading our article on the myth of the solid self.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link).

A New Kind of Relationship

Ultimately, working with the part that hates the body is not about fixing or changing it but about cultivating a new kind of relationship with it, a relationship that is based on compassion, curiosity, and a deep and abiding respect for its protective function. It is about recognizing that this part is not the enemy but a wounded aspect of ourselves that is in desperate need of our love and attention. It is about turning toward the pain, not away from it, and meeting it with a quality of presence that is both gentle and unwavering. That becomes not easy work. It requires courage, patience, and a willingness to feel the uncomfortable sensations that we have spent a lifetime avoiding. But it is in this turning toward, in this radical act of self-compassion, that the possibility of true healing emerges. We learn to listen to its whispers, to honor its rhythms, to celebrate its resilience. And in doing so, we begin to come home to ourselves, to the wholeness that has been there all along, waiting patiently for our return. This is the process of a lifetime, a work of reclaiming our own embodiment, of learning to live in a way that is both fully human and fully alive. It is a path that is available to each and every one of us, no matter how deep the rupture, no matter how long the exile. For those seeking to deepen their practice, we recommend exploring the resources available at Kalesh.love for a more full approach to embodied consciousness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I hate my body after trauma?

Hating your body after trauma is often a protective strategy. The body holds the physical and emotional sensations of the traumatic event, and by creating distance from it... a part of you is trying to protect you from re-experiencing that overwhelming pain. It's a learned response that equates the body with the danger it endured.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link).

How can I start to feel safe in my body again?

Feeling safe in your body again is a gradual process of rebuilding trust. It begins with small moments of neutral or pleasant sensation. Practices like noticing the feeling of your feet on the floor or the warmth of a cup in your hands can slowly teach your nervous system that the present moment is safe, even if the past was not.

What is the role of the nervous system in this?

The nervous system is central to this experience. Complex trauma dysregulates the nervous system, leaving it in a state of chronic hypervigilance or shutdown. The part that hates the body is often a reflection of a nervous system that is stuck in a defensive state, unable to access the "social engagement" system where feelings of safety and connection reside.

Will this feeling of hating my body ever go away?

The goal is not necessarily to make the feeling go away forever, but to change your relationship to it. Through practices of observation and compassion, the intensity of the hatred can diminish, and it no longer has to be the dominant voice in your internal world. It can become one part of your experience, rather than the whole of it.

A body tool that some find helpful for releasing chronic tension is Acupressure Mat and Pillow Set (paid link).

Think about that for a second.

Is there a specific practice you recommend for this?

A simple yet genuine practice is to place a hand on your heart, or another part of your body that feels relatively neutral, and simply notice the sensation of touch. You don't have to feel love or warmth, just the physical contact. This small act can begin to bridge the gap between the mind and the body, re-establishing a connection that may have been severed for a very long time.

For releasing tension along the spine, a tool worth considering is Chirp Wheel Back Roller (paid link).

The work of healing the relationship with your body is not about achieving a perfect state of self-love, but about the willingness to meet yourself where you are. What would it be like to approach the part that hates your body not as an adversary, but as a messenger? What might it have to tell you about what you have endured, and what you need to feel whole again?
